coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor for discrimination tyrosine enantiomers using graphene quantum dots and β-cyclodextrins composites
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Shuqing
Bi, Qing
Qiao, Chengdong
Sun, Yaming
Zhang, Xia
Lu, Xiaoquan
Zhao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemical sensor using the composites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and β-cyclodextrins (β-CDs) functionalized glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was developed for determination and recognition of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers which are biomarker of depression. The modified electrode is simple to fabricate and rapid, sensitive, selective to detect the Tyr enantiomers. In order to further validate the feasibility of the electrochemical sensor in real samples, the sensor was applied to the detection of L-Tyr in blood serum samples of healthy people and depression patients, and found that the quantities of L-Tyr of depression patients in serum is less than healthy people. The β-CDs-GQDs composites were fabricated as modification layer of electrodes. GQDs were used as substrate and functionalized with β-CDs. The β-CDs-GQDs composites utilized nanosize of GQDs and enantioselectivity of β-CDs to realize chiral recognition of Tyr. The β-CDs-GQDs modified electrode presented significant difference in the oxidation peak current with ratio of L to D-Tyr reaching 2.35. The detection limits of L-Tyr and D-Tyr were 6.07×10−9 M and 1.03×10−7 M, respectively and superior to detection limits of the reported methods. In addition, the stability and reproducibility of the prepared modified electrode were investigated, and achieved good results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Graphene quantum dots
β-cyclodextrin
Tyrosine enantiomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and β-CDs functionalized GQDs
Electrochemical measurements of the modified electrodes
The characterization of the GQDS/β-CDs hybrid
Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrodes
Enantioselective recognition of L- and D-Tyr
Analytical performance of the modified electrodes
Electrochemical measurements of Tyr enantiomers mixture
Reproducibility and stability of the electrochemical sensor
Detection of Tyr from serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR (A) and TEM (B) images of GQDs.

Cyclic voltammograms the Nyquist plots (A), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of different electrodes in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution (B), including bare GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b) and β-CDs-GQDs/GCE (c). (The equivalent circuit used to model impedance data: Rs, electrolyte resistance; Zw, Warburg impedance; Ret, electron-transfer resistance; Cdl, double-layer capacitance.) The scan rate is 0.1 V.s-1. Cyclic voltammograms (C) of different electrodes in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution including bare Au electrode (a), GQDs/Au electrode (b) and β-CDs-GQDs/Au electrode (c).
Cyclic voltammograms of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE in 1mM D-Tyr (a) and 1mM L-Tyr (b) solution. The scan rate is 0.1 Vs−1.
Influence of mass ratio of β-CDs and GQDs on the oxidation peak current of 1mM D-Tyr (a) and 1mM L-Tyr (b) in DPV.

Linear diagram of the percentage of L-Tye in 1mM Tyr and oxidation peak Current of CV of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE 1mM Tyr with different percentage of L-Tyr. Inset: Cyclic voltammograms of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE in 1mM Tyr with different percentage of L-Tyr.
Linear diagram of the concentration of L-Tyr (A) and D-Tyr (B) in solution and the oxidation peak current of CV of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE.
Cyclic voltammograms of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE in Met (A), Trp (B). 1mM D-Met (a) and 1mM L-Met (b) and 1mM D-Trp (a) and 1mM L-Trp (b). The scan rate is 0.1 V.s−1.
Comparison of different electrochemical chiral Tyr sensor for detection.
Comparison of the concentration of L-Tyr in serum samples of Healthy people and depression patients.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor for discrimination tyrosine enantiomers using graphene quantum dots and β-cyclodextrins composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, The Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, The Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Fist Hospital of Lanzhou University, Geriatrics Department Ward, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Fist Hospital of Lanzhou University, Geriatrics Department Ward

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemical sensor using the composites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and β-cyclodextrins (β-CDs) functionalized glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was developed for determination and recognition of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers which are biomarker of depression. The modified electrode is simple to fabricate and rapid, sensitive, selective to detect the Tyr enantiomers. In order to further validate the feasibility of the electrochemical sensor in real samples, the sensor was applied to the detection of L-Tyr in blood serum samples of healthy people and depression patients, and found that the quantities of L-Tyr of depression patients in serum is less than healthy people. The β-CDs-GQDs composites were fabricated as modification layer of electrodes. GQDs were used as substrate and functionalized with β-CDs. The β-CDs-GQDs composites utilized nanosize of GQDs and enantioselectivity of β-CDs to realize chiral recognition of Tyr. The β-CDs-GQDs modified electrode presented significant difference in the oxidation peak current with ratio of L to D-Tyr reaching 2.35. The detection limits of L-Tyr and D-Tyr were 6.07×10−9 M and 1.03×10−7 M, respectively and superior to detection limits of the reported methods. In addition, the stability and reproducibility of the prepared modified electrode were investigated, and achieved good results.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids were important enantiomers compounds, they also are the evaluating indicator for many diseases. Many studies also explored the potential relationship between amino acids enantiomers and several diseases, such as alzheimer’s disease and schizophrenia [9,10]. Tyrosine (Tyr) belong to enantiomers of aromatic amino acid. L-Tyr is essential amino acids, and serves as a precursor for the synthesis of several neurotransmitters including dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine and other catecholamines [1,2]. Hence, L-Tyr can help eliminate anxiety, irritability, and headache in the treatment. Now research has shown depression patients would have been associated with deficiencies of L-Tyr [3,4]. D-Tyr has identical physical and chemical properties with L-Tyr. But D-Tyr is non-essential amino acid, and serves as raw materials or synthetic block for preparation of medicaments [5–8]. Therefore, it is significant to recognize Tyr enantiomers and make clear D/L-Tyr relation with depression.

A lot of works have been devoted to identify and detect Tyr enantiomers. It is still difficult to simultaneously separate and detect the L-Tyr and D-Tyr due to the identical physical and chemical properties. Although, the mainly traditional detection methods for Tyr enantiomers are spectrographic methods, such as UV–Vis absorption spectrum [11,12], fluorescence spectrum [13] and circular dichroism spectra [14], the shortcoming of high cost and low sensitivity have not been overcome. Because of the simple operation, rapid detection, low sample quantity and high sensitivity, electrochemical methods can be evaluated as potential chiral analysis techniques [15–17]. Great efforts have already been made into recognize Tyr enantiomers. Zhou et al. analyzed the electrocatalytic ability of Tyr enantiomers on bare gold electrodes and the gold nanoparticles modified glassy carbon electrodes, and discussed the mechanism about the stereoselectivity in redox reactions of Tyr enantiomers on gold matrices [18]. Nie et al. designed an electrochemical senor based on cysteic acid (CyA) by electrochemical oxidation of L-cysteine, for enantioselective recognition of Tyr enantiomers, and this modified electrode showed significant peak potential difference in L- and D-Tyr solution [19]. It can be seen clearly that with the development of novel modified materials, chiral separation will achieve a broad prospect.

As the most commonly useful chiral selector, stable and high-inclusion β-CDs has proved its stereoselective discrimination for chiral recognition [20–23]. However, due to the poor conductivity and good water-solubility, it is difficulty for β-CDs immobilize on electrode surface directly, so limited the chiral recognition application of β-CDs in electrochemical field. Substrate materials were needed to form complexes with β-CDs and overcame the usage defection of β-CDs. Recently, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained much attentions for the enormous application potentials [24,25]. Owing to excellent photostability, biocompatibility and molecule size, GQDs have tremendous prospects in bioanalytical applications, such as bioimaging, biosensing and so on [26–28]. Zhao et al. had proposed that GQDs modified pyrolytic graphite electrode coupled with probe single-stranded DNA as electrochemical biosensors for the sensitive and selective detection of various single-stranded DNA or target protein molecules [29]. Roushani et al. had constructed an electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/riboflavin modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), utilized it to determine persulfate, and also observed high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide concentration range [30]. Small sizes, large specific surface area and abundant edge sites make GQDs advantageous for electrochemical sensing. Using GQDs as functional materials for the fabrication of chiral electrochemical sensors is still an innovative works. However, due to lack of enantioselectivity, GQDs need to be functionalized with chiral selector in electrochemical chiral sensor for enantiomers recognition. Based on the above statements, if β-CDs and GQDs composites modified electrode materials are applied for enantiomers recognition, the results could be expected.

In the paper, via utilizing the structures and properties complementarity of β-CDs and GQDs, we design, fabricate and perform a novel electrochemical biosensor based on β-CDs-GQDs composites on glassy carbon electrode (β-CDs-GQDs/GCE) for detection and discrimination tyrosine enantiomers. In set of experiments described in this report, sensitivity, linearity, selectivity and stability of the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE to Tyr enantiomers were determined. Moreover, the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE was applied to determination of serum Tyr of healthy people and depressions, and also achieved a good performance and satisfied results.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade and all solution were prepared using deionized (DI) water. L-Tyrosine (99%) and D-Tyrosine (98%) were purchased from Macklin. β-CDs was purchased from J&K Chemical. Purity of graphite powder was purchased from Sinopharm Group Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Potassium ferrocyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Sinoreagent.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were recorded between 550 and 4000cm−1 using an IFS120HR (Bruker, German). The morphologies of samples were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a TECNAI G2 TF20 (FEI, USA). Electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature (25 °C) using CHI660 electrochemical workstation. The sensor was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and (Differential Pulse Voltammetry) DPV. A three-electrode system consists of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) or modified electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a platinum wire as an auxiliary electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and β-CDs functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared from natural graphite by the acid treatment and chemical exfoliation. The method was reported previously with a little modification [31]. 1.0g purity of graphite powder was added into a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (80mL) and 65% HNO3 (20mL). The solution was refluxed for 30h at 140 °C. The mixture was cooled at room temperature and diluted with DI water (700mL). The pH was adjusted to 7 with 1M NaOH solution. The final product solution was further desalted by macroporous resin. The mixture was enriched to 400mL, and then poured into macroporous resins column with DI water immersion. Resins were immersed in mixture solution for 8h, and then DI water washing resins until SO4 2− could not be detected by BaCl2 in the eluent. After that, the eluent was collected, and dried to gain pure GQDs.
0.01g GQDs mixed with 0.10g β-CDs, and then added 5mL DI water to the mixture and made a sonication until it dissolved totally. The dispersion was β-CDs- GQDs composites solution.

For the modification process of the β-CDs-GQDs material, GCE surfaces were first polished with 1.0 and 0.05mm alumina slurry on a felt pad and washed with DI water. The GCE was then immersed in water and methanol for 5min in an ultrasonic bath, respectively in order to remove residual alumina particles by sonication. The electrode was dried at room temperature before the modification step. After drying, the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE was prepared by electrodeposition. The electrodeposition of β-CDs-GQDs composites films on the surface of the GCE was done by scanning in the potential range of −1.0–2.0 V vs Ag/AgCl at 0.1 V s−1 in GQDs: β-CDs (1:10) composites solution for 20 cycles. Then β-CDs-GQDs/GCE had been prepared. The preparation procedure of other mass rates of β-CDs-GQDs/GCE was same as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to characterize bare GCE, GQDs/GCE and β-CDs-GQDs/GCE, and all the electrochemical experiments were carried out with three electrode systems, in which the bare and modified GCE was used as working electrode, a platinum wire was applied as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode. CV measurements were carried out in potential range from −0.2 to 0.6 V in 5mM Fe(CN)6 4−/3− with 0.1M KCl at a scan rate of 0.1mV/s. The EIS measurements of the different surface modification of GCE electrodes were also obtained in 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution containing 0.1M KCl with the frequency ranging from 106 to 10−2 Hz and in a formal potential of 220mV at ambient temperature.

The solution containing suitable amounts of D and L-Tyr, together with 0.10M HCl solution with 0.10M KCl, were transferred to the electrochemical cell. CV measurements were carried out with the use of the three electrodes. The CVs were recorded by cycling the potential between +2.0 V and −1.0 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

For real sample analysis, the concentration of serum Tyr of health people and depression patients were examined by β-CDs-GQDs/GCE. At first, let 5mL fresh blood sample stand at room temperature for 2h, then blood divided into two layers, and the upper was serum. Collected the upper serum and diluted it to 5mL to form solution with 0.1M HCl and 0.1M KCl. Before the measurement process, the diluted serum samples were stored at −20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the GQDS/β-CDs hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: As observed in FT-IR image of GQDs ( Fig. 1A), GQDs has a large amount of oxygen containing groups. The spectral line of GQDs has obvious characteristic peak, the characteristic peaks at 3330, 1661cm−1 were assigned to O-H stretching vibrations and the C˭C stretching bands for aromatic rings. All the spectra of the graphene derivatives have the two characteristic bands. The band around 1729cm−1 corresponds to C˭O stretching vibrations from carbonyl and carboxylic groups. The bands around 1450cm−1 are attributed to C−OH stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectra provide the evidence of the presence of different types of oxygen functionalities on GQDs material. In the TEM image of GQDs (Fig. 1B), GQDs presented as round dots with uniform size distribution (average size of 2.5nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The modified electrode was characterized by CV and EIS. The redox peaks current of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- couple is extensively used as an electrochemical marker to determine the characteristic of electroactive materials. Fig. 2A displays the CVs of differently modified electrodes: bare GCE (curve a), GQDs/GCE (curve b) and β-CDs-GQDs/GCE (curve c) in 5mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1M KCl at 100mV/s. A couple of redox peaks can be observed in all CV plots. Bare GCE (curve a) showed that a quasi-reversible redox peaks with ΔEp =89mV and ΔIp =403µA. After GQDs were electrodeposited to the surface of GCE (curve b), ΔEp had a notably increase to 472mV, and ΔIp also decreased considerably to 191µA, which is likely due to repulsion between the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs and the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− [32,33]. After β-CDs-GQD composites modification on GCE (curve c), the ΔEp at the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE became relatively small (342mV) and ΔIp rose to 230µA compared with GQDs/GCE. This indicated that GQDs were wrapped around β-CDs, then oxygen-containing groups on GQDs were covered, so the repulsion to the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was reduced. The calculated areas of each modified electrodes also have been calculated for comparison. The integral areas of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE and β-CDs-GQDs/GCE were 1.508 Mc, 1.348 Mc and1.063 Mc. The integral areas of the modified electrodes revealed their capacitances, and also suggested the ability of electron transfer of bare GCE was waning with modified. EIS is used for further characterization of each step during GCE modification (Fig. 2B). For bare and modified GCE, the charge transfers resistance Rct, an indicator reflecting the resistance at the electrode-solution interface, is arranged in such an order: GQDs/GCE (824Ω, curve b) >β-CDs-GQDs/GCE (721Ω, curve c) > bare GCE (69Ω, curve a). The results of EIS agreed well with those of cyclic voltammograms. The same experiment with Au electrode as the base electrode has been attempted, the similar results has been presented in Fig. 2C. Since the stability of β-CD-GQDs/Au electrode was bad, GCE were selected for base electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselective recognition of L- and D-Tyr
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical responses of Tyr enantiomers on the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE were recorded by CV ( Fig. 3). D-Tyr (curve a) and L-Tyr (curve b) had oxidation peaks at 342mV and 172mV respectively. Owing to different steric hindrance during the combination with Tyr enantiomers, the β-CDs-GQDs preferentially combines with L-Tyr and results in larger peak current of L-Tyr than D-Tyr with a peak current ratio (IL/ID) of 2.35.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison of the recognition effects, several other electrodes including GQDs/GCE and different mass ratios of β-CDs-GQDs composites modified GCE were used for the control experiments. Due to DPV sensitivity, the DPV was used instead of CV to make the current changes submit obviously. The oxidation peak current of different electrodes in 1mM D-Tyr (curve a) and L-Tyr (curve b) were presented in Fig. 4. For GQDs/GCE (the β-CDs/GQDs=0), it is found that the two oxidation peaks of D-Tyr and L- Tyr were completely overlapped, revealing that GQDs didnot have the ability to distinguish the enantiomers [34]. With the increase of the amount of β-CDs in β-CDs-GQDs composites, the IL of L-Tyr increased linearly until that the mass ratios of β-CDs to GQDs achieved to 10 and in such a situation, the surface of GQDs had been saturated with β-CDs so as to the amount of β-CDs was unable to add. There was no noticeable change in the ID of D-Tyr. Hence, the IL/ID was obviously enlarged to 2.35. The findings showed that β-CDs had a better inclusion complexation to L-Tyr, which is also consistent with previous report [35]. The resolution mechanism of the racemates that can be largely attributed to the inclusion interactions. Meanwhile the synergistic effect of multiple interactions such as hydrophobic interaction, hydrogen bonding interaction may help to enhance the enantioresolution. Although L-Tyr and D-Tyr can deeply enter into the cavity of β-CDs, β-CDs-L-Tyr complex was more stable than β-CDs-D-Tyr owing to different steric hindrance and the stronger hydrogen bond interaction between β-CDs and L-Tyr. Thus, the peak current of β-CDs-L-Tyr changed evidently. Based on the above, it is no doubt that both GQDs and β-CDs play important roles in the improvement of chiral recognition efficiency. Because of excellent nanometer size, GQDs provided a substrate for immobilization of β-CDs. In one word, the synergetic effect of GQDs and β-CDs leads to a successful and effective chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements of Tyr enantiomers mixture
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the effect of the chiral electrochemical sensor, the total concentration of 1mML and D-Tyr with different EF values (enantiomeric fraction) of L-Tyr was detected. A series of CV curves corresponding to diﬀerent EF values of L-Tyr in an enantiomeric mixture were obtained (inset of Fig. 5). With the increase of the percentage of L-Tyr, the phenomena lower oxidation potential values decreased apparently. The peak current reveals a linear dependence of EF values with the correlation coefficients of 0.993 (Fig. 5).

The electrochemical response to a series of concentrations of L and D-Tyr was investigated in Fig. 6. L-Tyr had two calibration curves with different range of concentration. Over the range of 0.2–1.5mM, the peak currents of L-Tyr were linearly increased along to the concentration with regression equation of Ip (10−4A) =2.626c (mM) +5.143 (R2=0.995) (Fig. 6A). Another linear relationship between the peak currents and the concentration of L-Tyr from 6µM to 100µM with regression equation of Ip (10−4A) =35.229c (mM) +1.097 (R2=0.999) (Fig. 6A). The limit of detection based on S/N =3 was 6.07×10−9 M. Fig. 6B showed the peak currents were linearly increased with the concentration of D-Tyr in 0.1 – 1.0mM with regression equation of Ip (10−4A) =2.078c (mM) +1.170 (R2=0.990). The limit of detection based on S/N =3 was 1.03×10−7 M. From Table 1, it would observe that the present biosensor showed higher sensitivity and lower detection limit in detection of Tyr enantiomers compared to previous works.

The determination of amino acids and metabolites in biological fluids such as plasma and urine is still a very challenging task. The largest difficulty in this area is often a large amount of distractors in biological fluids. In order to investigate the selectivity of the electrochemical sensor to Tyr enantiomers, under optimized experimental conditions described above, nine available amino acids enantiomers, including tryptophan, glutamine, threonine, alanine, arginine, valine, serine, methionine, lysine, had no obvious oxidation peak except for methionine (Met) and tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers ( Fig. 7). The β-CDs-GQDs/GCE showed similar responses to Met (Fig. 7A) and Trp (Fig. 7B) enantiomers with small red-ox peak. However, Met and Trp enantiomers can be oxidized at 0.8–0.9 V which is far away from the oxidation potential of Tyr enantiomers (0.1–0.4 V), so this influence could be neglected. All these studies indicated that the prepared sensor had good selectivity for the determination of Tyr enantiomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility was estimated with six β-CDs-GQDs/GCEs that were fabricated independently by the same procedure. The RSD is 4.85% for peak current measuring in 1mM L-Tyr which demonstrates the reliability of the fabrication procedure. Additionally, the stability of one β-CDs-GQDs/GCE was also investigated. After 10 days, the current response is approximately 98.31% of the original value. The excellent long-term stability and reproducibility of the modified electrodes make them attractive in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Tyr from serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the feasibility of the method for Tyr quantitation in real sample, the serum samples of 10 healthy people and 9 depression patients have been processed for detection. These depression patients had similar tends and the same treatment regimen. The results were presented in Table 2. Because the main existing form of amino acids in blood was L-amino acids, the measured effects were converted to the concentration of L-Tyr. The normal range of serum Tyr was 33–83μM [36]. As shown in Table 2, the serum Tyr of healthy people and depression patients are in normal range, and the average of depression patients was lower than healthy people, which was consistent with previous reports that deficiencies of L-Tyr in human would have been associated with depression. Another reason of this result may be that depression patients had more amount of D-Tyr, and the D-Tyr would lead to a lower current response.

Tyr play regulatory roles in both metabolism and the control of cell proliferation. Specifically, in people elevated serum Tyr levels occur with some metabolic disease. In this work, an electrochemical sensor for determination and discrimination Tyr enantiomers was presented. Owing to the nanoscale of GQDs as substrate and the specific recognition of β-CDs to Tyr enantiomers, the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE showed a very sensitive and selective platform for determination and discrimination Tyr enantiomers. The electrochemical sensor is simple to prepare, and fast to discriminate, it offers a reliable and effective detection for Tyr enantiomers in practical samples. If the more details and mechanisms studies can be performed, the β-CDs-GQDs/GCE would be applied further for clinical screening and diagnosis with great significance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support to the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant: 21405161, 21675163).
